sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

cross_connections.display_label: His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.

H00525.prominent_name_reason: Wife of Gov. Caleb Strong — 6th and 10th Governor of Massachusetts (1800-1807, 1812-1816), U.S. Senator, Founding Father, delegate to the Philadelphia Convention of 1787. Daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton (H00264). 5th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker.
H00525.gravestone_inscription: In Memory of Mrs. Sarah Strong, the wife of Caleb Strong and daughter of the Rev John Hooker who having early devoted herself to the service of her Redeemer and through life exemplified the virtues & graces of his religion gently breathed out her spirit in humble reliance upon his power and love on the 12th day of February AD 1817 in the 60th year of her age.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of smallpox before her wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. John Hooker pastor of Northampton MA died of smallpox in February 1777 when she was 19. Caleb Strong, a lawyer who had joined her father's congregation five years earlier, married her in November 1777 — nine months after the funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Matriarch of the Strong-Hooker-Dwight branch
narrative_blocks.body: Mother of nine children with Gov. Caleb Strong including Clarissa Strong Dwight, who married Timothy Dwight Jr. in 1809 and produced the New Haven Dwight-Lyman cluster — three sons of whom were Yale-educated and one of whom Sam's tree records as ancestor of two generations of Yale presidents.

classification.lineage_note: H00526 Seth (gen 5) → H00264 Rev. John (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

compiler_connection.notes: On the compiler's paternal (Hooker surname) line. Marriage to Abigail Gay explains the middle name 'Gay' in the compiler's father Michael Gay Hooker. The Gay family farm at Hinsdale NH became the Hooker place.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: Seth(H00526) → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker

open_questions.question: Father Rev. John Hooker of Northampton — EH entry number
open_questions.notes: Son of Rev. John and Sarah (Worthington) Hooker of Northampton, MA. Rev. John descends through H00098 (John No. 2, b. 1695). Need EH entry number.
open_questions.action: Look up Rev. John Hooker of Northampton in EH index

military_service.notes: EH: 'though very young he had some service' — born 1759, so approximately age 15-18 during service

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Came to the Hinsdale homestead in 1789 through his marriage to Abigail Gay; worked the farm and made hats there for fifty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage carried Hookers north to Hinsdale, 1789
narrative_blocks.body: Born and raised at Northampton, he served briefly in the Revolution as a teenager and learned hatmaking. His 1789 marriage to Abigail Gay of Hinsdale brought him onto her father Bunker's homestead, establishing the Hooker family on what became their New Hampshire seat for four generations.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1782, Springfield lawyer and judge
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1782 and settled at Springfield, MA, where he practiced law and rose to Judge of the Court of Common Pleas and then Judge of Probate at his death. He was a Deacon of the First Congregational Church of Springfield throughout this period.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of American Board for Foreign Missions
narrative_blocks.body: He was an active member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), the first US foreign-missions organization, founded 1810 in Williamstown MA. The Board sent New England missionaries to India, the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii), and Native American tribes — a defining institution of New England Federalist evangelical culture.

career.notes: Practiced medicine at Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #529. Established the Hooker physician tradition in Westhampton continued by son Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414, 1799-1869) and through several Westhampton descendants.
Per EH 1909 #529: 'He was a physician and also a trader at West Hampton, Mass.' — supplementing physician income with a trading operation, common pattern for rural New England doctors of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-four as Westhampton physician
narrative_blocks.body: Born Northampton MA 1766; died Westhampton MA February 27, 1861 at age ninety-four — extraordinary longevity spanning the early Republic through the Civil War's outbreak. Practiced medicine in Westhampton MA and also operated as a trader; both occupations supported a large family of seven children with first wife Anna Thompson before her 1810 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife at forty-two, remarried after two years
narrative_blocks.body: First wife Anna Thompson died Westhampton April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving him with seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. William remarried 1812 to Rhoda Phelps, widow of Ensign Hartland of Hartland Connecticut. No documented children from the second marriage; Rhoda predeceased William by fourteen years.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #530: 'an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vt.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apothecary and grocer at Rutland; wife predeceased him
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker was an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vermont, where he spent his adult life and died in 1836 at sixty-five. His wife Sarah Brown died four years before him in 1832, predeceasing him despite being nine years younger.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of Col. John Brown of Pittsfield
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. If confirmed, Thomas Hooker married the daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons: one to Boston missions
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and Henry Brown Hooker were born the same day — August 31, 1802 — and their lives diverged completely: John moved to Chicago in 1836 among the city's earliest New England settlers, while Henry graduated Middlebury College, entered the ministry, and became Secretary of Missions at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of five children died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 and died there in 1866; his sister Julia Ann married a Rutland lawyer and also died in Chicago in 1842. Two of Thomas Hooker's five children ended their days in the city that barely existed when they were born.

narrative_blocks.header: Amiableness of the friend, gentleness of the mother
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1812 Northampton obituary described Lucy Hooker Ashmun as a woman of uncommon merits: 'the amiableness of the friend, the cheerful, mild disposition of the wife, the gentleness of the mother.' She died after a long illness at thirty-seven, leaving five children — the eldest just twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Died four years before Eli reached the Senate
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Hooker Ashmun died in 1812, four years before her husband Eli Porter Ashmun was elected to the United States Senate — she never saw the political career that would make his name. Their son George and son John Hooker Ashmun would go on to federal office and Harvard Law, carrying the legacy she did not live to witness.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Northampton's Rev. John Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy was the daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton, Massachusetts — a great-granddaughter in the line descending from Rev. Thomas Hooker who founded Hartford in 1636. She married into the Ashmun family in 1798, and her middle name Hooker would be carried by her daughter and then her granddaughter across two more generations.

primary_residence.notes: Seth Hooker of Kensington — hatter and farmer on the Gay place at Hinsdale NH

bio.notes: FVR spells name 'Marah Burnham'; EH gives 'Sarah Burnham'

classification.lineage_note: H00533 William (gen 5) → H00265 Seth (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

compiler_connection.notes: William Hooker is the compiler's direct paternal ancestor through the Hooker surname line. His father Seth (H00265) died when William was 2 years old. William is the link between the confirmed paternal line and the compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker. Research needed to trace descendants.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: H00533 William → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker (compiler)

open_questions.question: Full descent from William Hooker (H00533) to Michael Gay Hooker
open_questions.notes: William born 1756 Kensington CT; died 1826. His starred children H01427-H01433 are the next research step. The compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker's connection runs through one of these children.
open_questions.action: Research EH entries 1427-1433; check CT vital records and census records for William's children

primary_residence.notes: Born and died Kensington CT. Also had family connection to Hinsdale NH through wife.

cross_connections.display_label: Her daughter Amelia married my son Dr. William in 1814.
His daughter Amelia married my son Dr. William in 1814.
was the father of his wife Hannah Jones

military_service.unit: Capt. Caleb Bull's Company in Webb's Regiment, Continental Army
military_service.notes: Enlisted Apr 21, 1777 as Private for 3 years in Capt. Caleb Bull's Company, Webb's Regiment. Promoted Sergeant May 16, 1780 for an additional 8 months. Member of the Hartford 'band of music' (eight musicians) raised 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line. Pensioned Nov 4, 1818. DAR Ancestor #A057709.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford band of music attached to Connecticut line
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was a member of the eight-man 'band of music' raised in Hartford in 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line of the Continental Army — one of the rare documented Revolutionary War regimental musical ensembles. The band provided drums, fifes, and field music during the war's middle years. The arrangement was unusual: most Revolutionary regiments had ad hoc field musicians rather than a formally organized eight-piece band.

narrative_blocks.header: Three years Private, eight months Sergeant 1777-1780
narrative_blocks.body: William enlisted April 21, 1777 as a Private in Captain Caleb Bull's Company of Webb's Regiment for a three-year term. On May 16, 1780 he was promoted to Sergeant for an additional eight months of service. He was pensioned November 4, 1818 (DAR Ancestor #A057709) — the federal pension secured nearly four decades after his service ended.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children with Sarah Wilcox of Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: William married Sarah Wilcox of Berlin Connecticut and they had nine documented children together. The family remained in Kensington, the original Berlin Connecticut parish where William had been born in 1756 and where he would die in 1826. The nine-child household was characteristic of the post-Revolutionary Connecticut farming generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Kensington Berlin CT 1756 to 1826 age sixty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was born September 15, 1756 in Kensington (now Berlin) Connecticut and died February 4, 1826 in Kensington at age sixty-nine. He is buried at West Lane Cemetery in Berlin Connecticut. His Revolutionary War service spanned the war's middle period from the bleakest 1777 Valley Forge winter through the war's final 1780-1781 phase.

cross_connections.display_label: Urania Hooker married Matthew Hart — continuing the Hart-Hooker intermarriage.
Urania Hooker married Matthew Hart — continuing the Hart-Hooker intermarriage.

bio.notes: Widow of Samuel Seymour of Kensington before marrying Elijah Hooker

career.location: Morristown, St. Lawrence County, New York
career.notes: Bought Arnold Smith hotel fall 1815. Sold to Alfred Hooker Aug 23, 1823.
First collector of port. Overseer of the poor when town organized March 27, 1821.

narrative_blocks.header: St. Lawrence River hotel owner; first port collector
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker moved his family from Turin to Morristown in the fall of 1815 — a village of only a few houses on the St. Lawrence River — where he bought Arnold Smith's hotel and ran it until 1823. When the town was organized on March 27, 1821, he was appointed overseer of the poor and became its first collector of the port.

narrative_blocks.header: From Connecticut to Turin to the St. Lawrence
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker left Kensington, Connecticut after his first wife Abigail Goodrich died in 1794, remarried Mary Ives in 1795, and removed the family to Turin, New York in 1802 — then in 1814 to Morristown, beautifully situated on the St. Lawrence River, where he died April 12, 1829.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-widow in the General Hart house thirty-eight years
cross_connections.notes: After Cyprian Hart died March 1, 1806 and General Selah Hart died June 10, 1806 in the same house, Lucy (then 34) and Ruth Cole Hart (then 63) — wives of nephew and adoptive uncle — kept the General Hart homestead together for as long as they lived. Ruth died there January 15, 1844 at 101 years, 2 months, 16 days. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying July 8, 1851 at 79. EH #537: 'his widow and the widow of his uncle Gen. Selah Hart occupied the General Hart house and property together as long as they lived.'

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935595.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-four with six young children
narrative_blocks.body: She lost her husband Cyprian Hart on March 1, 1806 and his adoptive uncle General Selah Hart fourteen weeks later on June 10. By the adoption arrangement she remained mistress of the General Hart homestead with her six children, the youngest only nine months old. She had all six baptized at Kensington church the following November.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-five years a widow, three children unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: Of her six children, three died unmarried before their forty-second year: Selah at forty-one in 1838, the physician Henry Cyprian at thirty in 1831, and Emily at twenty-nine in 1835. She herself died July 8, 1851 at Kensington aged seventy-nine — having outlived her husband by forty-five years and having shared the General Hart house with her co-widow Ruth Cole Hart for thirty-eight of them.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up in his father Elijah's Kensington house.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed six months after his first wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Griswold, whom he married in 1808, died about six months later. He then followed his brother John to upstate New York, settling at Carthage, where he kept a large country store and served more than twenty years as postmaster.

cross_connections.display_label: co-wrote his schoolbooks and founded America's deaf school

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Hartford minister, editor, and children's author

institutions.institution_blurb: Chaplain at end of life; died there December 17, 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: He continued Gallaudet's Bible series after Gallaudet died
narrative_blocks.body: In collaboration with Rev. Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet — the pioneer of American deaf education who co-founded the American School for the Deaf in Hartford — Horace Hooker co-authored The Practical Spelling Book and The School and Family Dictionary, two educational works that circulated widely in New England schools and were reprinted under both names for decades after Gallaudet's death in 1851. When Gallaudet died, Hooker continued the Scripture Biographies for the Young series his collaborator had begun — completing it as an act of literary stewardship that kept both names in circulation in Connecticut's religious education networks through the Civil War era, and that reflected his role as one of Hartford's most productive clerical authors across four decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of CT Missionary Society for twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: For more than twenty years he served as secretary of the Connecticut Missionary Society. He ended his life as chaplain of the Hartford insane retreat — where he died December 17, 1864, having ministered there until the end.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding student of Yale's theological department
narrative_blocks.body: As a Yale tutor in 1817 he joined the small group of students who studied theology informally under Professor Eleazar T. Fitch — the cohort that became the founding class of what grew into Yale Divinity School. The twelve students formalized the program by inviting Professor Kingsley to teach Hebrew and Professor Goodrich elocution and sermon composition, transforming twice-weekly evenings at Fitch's house into Yale's first theological department.

children_noted: I00041 Mercy/Mary Hart — married John Hooker Jr. (H00098) July 4, 1728

open_questions.question: Deacon Thomas Hart vs Deacon Ebenezer Hart — identity of Mercy Hart's father
open_questions.notes: EH entry 98 gives Mercy Hart's father as 'Deacon Thomas Hart'. FVR (Barbour Collection) gives 'John, Jr., m. Mary HEART, d. Dea. Ebenezer' — suggesting Deacon Ebenezer Hart. Could be two different Deacons, or EH/FVR error on first name. Andrews' Stephen Hart and his Descendants should resolve this.
open_questions.action: Consult Andrews' Stephen Hart genealogy; check Kensington church deacon records

sources.full: Alfred Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart and his Descendants (New Britain, CT), pp. 364-5

church_roles.ordination_charge: I charge thee before God and the Lord Jesus Christ... that thou be faithful to the trust that is committed unto thee; thou art made a steward of the external good things of this Church.

notable.notable_blurb: Farmington militia captain, General Court speaker

cross_connections.display_label: shared with him the leadership of early Farmington

bio.title: Captain
bio.bio_blurb: Farmington militia captain; General Court Speaker

narrative_blocks.header: One of Farmington’s two prominent men
narrative_blocks.body: Contemporaries paired him with John Hooker as “the two prominent men of Farmington” — ensign to captain of the trainband, Speaker of the General Court, and seventeen years a Hartford County justice. His son Deacon Thomas married into the Hooker family.

burialCemetery.name: Christian Lane Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Kensington is a parish of Berlin, CT — sources may list burial location as either 'Kensington' or 'Berlin'. John Hooker No. 2 (H00098) and Mercy Hart Hooker (I00041) buried here. Burial site of Samuel Hooker (H00086) and family
